1: The Spirit-anointing oracle of Isaiah 61:1-3 in its original context speaks of a prophetic figure commissioned to announce the Year of Jubilee (Lev 25) — the release of captives, the year of divine favor, the comfort of mourners. Whether Isa 61 belongs to 'Deutero-Isaiah' or 'Trito-Isaiah' (Isaiah 56-66), its Jubilee-economics for the poor were already a live text in Second Temple Judaism — Qumran's Melchizedek Scroll (11QMelch) applies Isa 61 to the eschatological release in the final Jubilee under Melchizedek. Jesus's Nazareth reading (Luke 4:18-21) claims to be this eschatological Jubilee-fulfillment: his ministry is the Year of the Lord's favor enacted in person.
2: To proclaim the year of the LORD's favor and the day of vengeance of our God — to comfort all who mourn. The year of favor (shenat ratzon YHWH) is the Jubilee vocabulary: the fiftieth year of release and restoration (Leviticus 25:10). The day of vengeance pairs the restorative and judicial aspects of the same divine act. Luke 4:18-20 records Jesus reading the Isaiah 61:1-2 scroll in the Nazareth synagogue and stopping mid-sentence — he announces the year of favor but does not read the day of vengeance, declaring that the favorable year is now fulfilled but withholding the judgment announcement.
3: To bestow on those who mourn in Zion — a garland instead of ashes, the oil of joy instead of mourning, the garment of praise instead of a spirit of despair. The triple reversal of ANE mourning customs: ashes on the head was the act of communal grief after catastrophe (2 Samuel 13:19; Jeremiah 6:26); the garland (pe'er) was the festive headpiece of celebration. Mourning oil was replaced by joy-oil; sackcloth was replaced by a garment of praise. The community renamed oaks of righteousness (eilei ha-tzedek) becomes YHWH's planting — a permanent, rooted, fruitful tree rather than a cut-down stump.
4: They will rebuild the ancient ruins — they will raise up the former desolations; they will restore the devastated cities, the ruins of many generations. The rebuilding task is both literal (the physical reconstruction of the ruined Judean cities after the Babylonian destruction) and symbolic (the restoration of covenant community across the generational gap of exile). Ruins of many generations points to the accumulated desolation of successive conquests — Assyrian deportations of the north, then Babylonian destruction of the south.
5: Strangers will stand and tend your flocks — foreigners will be your farmers and your vinedressers. The reversal of Israel's labor status: in Egypt they were enslaved agricultural workers; in Babylon they were displaced non-citizens; in the restoration the nations perform the agricultural work while Israel assumes the priestly role. This is not ethnic supremacism but the fulfillment of Israel's priestly vocation — freed from economic survival to serve as the covenant intermediaries between YHWH and the nations.
6: But you will be called the Priests of the LORD — you will be named the Ministers of our God; you will eat the wealth of the nations and in their glory you will boast. The entire community designated as priests (kohanim YHWH) and ministers (mesharetei Elohenu) democratizes the priestly role to all of Israel — a fulfillment of Exodus 19:6 (you will be for me a kingdom of priests). 1 Peter 2:9 extends this to the church: a royal priesthood, a holy nation. Eating the wealth of the nations (rather than paying tribute to them) is the economic reversal of the covenant curse.
7: Instead of shame you will receive a double portion — instead of dishonor you will rejoice in your inheritance; therefore you will possess a double portion in your land, and everlasting joy will be yours. The double portion is the firstborn's inheritance right in Israelite law (Deuteronomy 21:17): Elisha asked for the double portion of Elijah's spirit. The exilic community stripped of land-inheritance receives not merely restoration but doubling — the firstborn's portion rather than the disinherited child's nothing. The shame-to-double-portion reversal is a comprehensive honor-restoration.
8: For I, YHWH, love justice — I hate robbery and wrongdoing; I will faithfully give them their reward and will make an everlasting covenant with them. YHWH's justice-love (ohev mishpat) is the divine character that grounds the covenant promise: the God who hates robbery will not allow his people to remain robbed of their inheritance. The everlasting covenant (brit olam) echoes the Noahic (Genesis 9:16), Abrahamic (Genesis 17:7), and Davidic (2 Samuel 23:5) covenants — the new covenant is placed in that sequence.
9: Their descendants will be known among the nations and their offspring among the peoples — all who see them will recognize that they are a people the LORD has blessed. International recognition of the covenant community's blessed status is the social proof of covenant restoration. In the ANE, visible prosperity and security were interpreted as evidence of divine favor; the exile community's misery was interpreted by neighbors as evidence of YHWH's rejection. The reversal will be equally legible: their visible flourishing will declare YHWH's blessing to observers.
10: I will greatly rejoice in YHWH — my soul will exult in my God, for he has clothed me with the garments of salvation and wrapped me in the robe of righteousness, as a bridegroom adorns himself with a garland, and as a bride adorns herself with her jewels. The speaker (the Servant or the restored community) voices the exuberance of covenant restoration through three clothing metaphors: garments of salvation, robe of righteousness, and the wedding-day adornment of bride and groom. Wedding ornamentation was the most elaborate personal display in the ANE — it marks the most festive possible social transition.
11: For as the earth brings forth its sprouts and as a garden makes what is planted in it spring up, so the Lord GOD will make righteousness and praise spring up before all the nations. The seed-germination analogy for the inevitability of the covenant's fulfillment: what YHWH planted (righteousness and praise in the community) will grow as certainly as seeds sprout in watered ground. The agricultural metaphor grounds the eschatological hope in the reliable processes of creation — YHWH's word in the community is as certain as the spring harvest.
